top_categories.workout_context: 14,009ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
At mile 60 of 80, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
La Stelvio Santini's alpine mountain passes — HC climbs forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 80 miles.
Race-pace efforts that mimic La Stelvio Santini's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before June.
173ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 80 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
At La Stelvio Santini, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
14,009ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of La Stelvio Santini. Train it until it feels boring.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At La Stelvio Santini, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on alpine mountain passes — HC climbs. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
The aerobic base that lets you ride alpine mountain passes — HC climbs at tempo for 80 miles without cracking. No shortcuts.

race_overlay.heat: June in Bormio → Summit of Passo dello Stelvio — Bormio valley 15-20°C, Stelvio summit can drop to near-freezing. 15-20°C temperature differential. HIGH weather risk at 2,757m — thunderstorms, rain, sleet, snow possible in early June. Begin heat acclimatization 10–14 days before La Stelvio Santini — 20–30min sauna sessions or midday rides in full kit. Pre-load sodium 48 hours out. Target 500–750ml/hr with electrolytes on race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for La Stelvio Santini’s 80 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: La Stelvio Santini with 14,009ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. Summit at 2,757m — severe wind chill on exposed upper switchbacks; Temperature can drop 15-20°C between Bormio and summit.
race_overlay.terrain: Highly technical terrain at La Stelvio Santini demands practice on similar surfaces. Ride alpine mountain passes — hc climbs at race-day cadence weekly. Practice cornering, descending, and power delivery on unstable surfaces. Dial in tire pressure before race week — 5 PSI wrong costs you 15+ minutes over 80 miles.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on VO2max, Durability, and Gravel Specific to prepare you for 80 miles of alpine mountain passes — hc climbs in Bormio → Summit of Passo dello Stelvio.
